summary: Powerful Estimated Prophet‑UJB‑China sequence with soaring Morning Dew
blurb: The Richmond show delivers a tight Estimated Prophet > Uncle John's Band > China Doll suite and an out‑of‑space Morning Dew that lifts the entire second set. Jerry’s guitar is on fire, Phil drops massive bass bombs, and Brent’s harmonies add extra sparkle.
review: Richmond Coliseum on November 2, 1985 captured one of the Dead’s quintessential ’85 outings. The night opens with a raucous first‑set that builds through Bertha, Great­est Story Ever Told and a gritty “C.C. Rider” into Althea, but it’s the second set that truly shines. A crisp Estimated Prophet segues straight into Uncle John’s Band and then rockets into a searing China Doll – a transition reviewers call “studio‑quality” in its fluidity. The ensuing Drum/Space segment bursts into an out‑of‑space Morning Dew where Phil’s bass bombs shake the arena, while Jerry delivers his sharpest second solo of the night. Throwing Stones and Turn On Your Lovelight close the set with unbridled energy, and a heartfelt “It’s All Over Now Baby Blue” encore rounds out a memorable performance. Across all sources the band is described as locked‑in: Jerry’s tone perfect and forceful, Phil’s low end both powerful and tastefully restrained, Brent’s keyboard flourishes and vocal harmonies lifting the groove, and Mickey (with Bill) driving a “buzzing, clicking” rhythm that defines the 1985 sound. The consensus is clear – this show is a high‑energy highlight of the late‑’80s era, especially for anyone chasing that legendary Estimated Prophet‑UJB‑China chain and an unforgettable Morning Dew.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Nearly all recordings are marked positive, with multiple reviewers praising the same standout moments – Estimated Prophet > Uncle John's Band > China Doll, soaring Morning Dew, and high‑energy drum/space work. No consistent negative comments about playing quality appear; only isolated song‑specific critiques that do not affect overall show perception.

best_recording.reason: Crystal‑clear soundboard capture with excellent mix, minimal hiss and the most faithful representation of the band’s dynamics.

key_highlights: Estimated Prophet → Uncle John's Band → China Doll sequence feels like a studio take in a live setting.
Morning Dew delivers an out‑of‑space climax with Phil’s massive bass bomb and Jerry’s soaring solo.
Drum/Space segment after China Doll is a powerful, buzzing showcase of the ’85 rhythm section.
Brent’s vocal harmonies and keyboard flourishes add extra sparkle throughout the set.

band_performance.Jerry: Sharp picking, perfect tone and a forceful second solo on Uncle John’s Band; his soaring lead on Morning Dew is described as ‘on fire’ and the guitar work after China Doll as ready to roll a fatty.
band_performance.Phil: Delivers powerful bass bombs – especially during Dew – and adds dynamic depth throughout; praised for both massive low‑end impact and tasteful restraint when needed.
band_performance.Bob: Slide work shines on Aiko and other numbers, providing flavor despite occasional lyrical slip‑ups; vocal delivery is strong and energetic.
band_performance.Brent: Provides soaring vocal harmonies and keyboard flourishes that lift Estimated Prophet and the Iko vibe; his playing is noted as ‘ass‑off’ on big moments and adds texture throughout.
band_performance.Mickey: Drums drive the set with a buzzing, clicking signature of ’85; the post‑China Doll drum segment is highlighted as a musical high point.
band_performance.Billy: Solid partnership with Mickey, delivering steady propulsion that underpins the second‑set jams and keeps the energy relentless.
